title: Letters From Christ
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: Letters From Christ_ 2 Corinthians 3_1-6 (Pastor Tito Lyro).txt

outline.title: Truth 1: The Redeemed Christian Is a Letter of Commendation to the Gospel (2 Cor. 3:1-3)
subpoints: The Corinthian church wanted Paul to prove his apostolic credentials; Paul says they themselves are his letter of recommendation
The changed lives of former fornicators, thieves, homosexuals, and drunkards at Corinth are the proof that Paul's gospel is true (1 Corinthians 6:9-11)
We are letters from Christ written by the Spirit — our lives commend or misrepresent Christ to the watching world
Paul's letter of commendation was written in his heart — he continued to minister lovingly to a church that challenged him

outline.title: Truth 2: The Redeemed Christian Is Changed at the Heart Level (2 Cor. 3:3)
subpoints: Paul connects three Old Testament images: tablets of stone, tablets of flesh, and the Spirit — all pointing to the new covenant
Jeremiah 31 promises a new covenant: God's law written on the heart, not stone
Ezekiel 36 describes the mechanism: God gives a new heart, removes the heart of stone, puts His Spirit within
The Psalm 40 psalmist anticipates the new covenant: 'I delight to do your will, O God, and your law is within my heart'
The Spirit's work through the gospel produces not external compliance but heart-level delight in obedience

outline.title: Truth 3: The Redeemed Christian's Confidence Is in Christ Alone (2 Cor. 3:4-5)
subpoints: Paul's confidence in his ministry does not rest on his eloquence or skill — he came in weakness, fear, and trembling
The church's spiritual destiny does not depend on the pastor's abilities — thanks be to God
Sufficiency in the biblical sense is not 'the minimum needed' but 'all that is needed' — Christ is all-sufficient
Philippians 3: Paul counts all his credentials as rubbish compared to gaining Christ and being found in Him
The question is not what we can offer God but who we are trusting — ourselves or Christ

outline.title: Truth 4: The Redeemed Christian Has Life by the Spirit (2 Cor. 3:6)
subpoints: The letter (law) kills any pretense of self-sufficiency and drives us to Christ; the Spirit gives life
The false teachers in Corinth were likely teaching that law-keeping was necessary to stand before God — the letter apart from the Spirit
The law is not bad; it is misused when treated as a ladder to earn God's favor rather than a mirror showing our need for Christ
When the law kills our pride, we are ready to receive the life that the Spirit gives through the gospel
Those who rely on their own righteousness have no hope; those who die to self find life in Christ

summary:
This sermon on 2 Corinthians 3:1-6 examines Paul's response to the Corinthian church's demand for his apostolic credentials. Paul's answer is striking: rather than producing letters of recommendation, he points the Corinthians to their own transformed lives as the evidence that his gospel is the true gospel of Jesus Christ. The sermon develops four truths from this passage, each centered on the sufficiency of Christ.

First, the redeemed Christian is a letter of commendation to the gospel. The changed lives of the Corinthians — once fornicators, thieves, homosexuals, drunkards — are irrefutable evidence that Paul's message was from Christ. Similarly, every believer bears Christ's name and commends or misrepresents Him by the way they live, even in their failures, because the grace of a God who forgives on His worst day as on His best remains unchanged.

Second, the change that comes through the gospel is at the heart level, not superficial. Paul's imagery in verse 3 deliberately echoes the new covenant promises of Jeremiah 31 and Ezekiel 36: a new heart of flesh replacing a heart of stone, the Spirit dwelling within and causing God's people to delight in His law rather than merely obey it externally.

Third, the believer's confidence is entirely in Christ. Paul did not come with eloquence or persuasive wisdom. The church's spiritual health does not depend on the pastor's gifts. Christ is sufficient — not in the minimal sense but in the all-encompassing sense that He is all that is needed. Fourth, life comes through the Spirit who works through the proclaimed Word. The law kills self-sufficiency and drives the sinner to Christ; the Spirit gives life through that same gospel. The proper use of the law is not as a ladder to righteousness but as a mirror exposing need, preparing the sinner to receive the life only the Spirit can give.
transcript:
## Introduction: Paul's Credentials

In cop shows, detectives always flash their badge to prove they are really cops. Somehow that badge opens all the doors — it verifies that you are who you say you are. The Corinthian Christians wanted to see Paul's badge. They wanted Paul to prove he was worthy of being listened to, that he was a genuine apostle of Jesus Christ. This likely happened because false teachers had come into the church and encouraged the Corinthians to challenge Paul's authority — not by challenging what he was teaching, since that was obviously true, but by challenging his credentials to proclaim those things.

As we consider what Paul says about his credentials in 2 Corinthians 3:1–6, I want to see four truths. First, the redeemed Christian is a letter of commendation to the gospel of Jesus Christ. Second, the redeemed Christian is changed at the heart level. Third, the redeemed Christian's confidence is in the Christ of the gospel and in nothing else. And fourth, the redeemed Christian has life by the Spirit.

## 1. The Redeemed Christian Is a Letter of Commendation (vv. 1–3)

> *"Do we begin again to commend ourselves? Or do we need, as some others, epistles of commendation to you or letters of commendation from you? You are our epistle written in our hearts, known and read by all men; clearly you are an epistle of Christ, ministered by us, written not with ink but by the Spirit of the living God, not on tablets of stone but on tablets of flesh, that is, of the heart."* (2 Corinthians 3:1–3)

Didn't we already go through this? Paul asks. Of all the churches, the one that least needed proof of his apostleship was the one that had most benefited from his ministry. If they want letters of commendation — reference letters that prove he is who he says he is — they should look in the mirror.

*You are our epistle.* The Corinthian Christians *are* the proof that Paul proclaimed the true gospel. They are the best reference letter he could have. And everyone could see it — they were letters from Christ, readable by all.

The former fornicators, the former idolaters, the former adulterers, the former homosexuals, the former thieves, the former drunkards — these are the letters written by Christ that prove the truth of Paul's message. Paul himself writes in 1 Corinthians 6:9–11 one of the most powerful passages in the New Testament:

> *"Do you not know that the unrighteous will not inherit the kingdom of God? Do not be deceived. Neither fornicators, nor idolaters, nor adulterers, nor homosexuals, nor sodomites, nor thieves, nor covetous, nor drunkards, nor revilers, nor extortioners will inherit the kingdom of God. And such were some of you. But you were washed, but you were sanctified, but you were justified in the name of the Lord Jesus and by the Spirit of our God."* (1 Corinthians 6:9–11)

*Such were some of you.* Were. Past tense. They are no longer who they were. They have been washed, sanctified, justified. Even with all their continued faults — and they still struggled with many things — they were considered epistles of Christ, written by the Holy Spirit.

If we have been born again, we remain letters of Christ written by the Spirit, even in our failures. Our lives point to Christ and His grace — the One who forgives us for His own sake and who does not love us any differently on our worst day than on our best day, because His love is not based on our obedience or righteousness but on the Lord Jesus Christ, who is the same yesterday, today, and forever.

Paul Barnett, in his commentary on 2 Corinthians, writes: *"It is one thing to possess the appropriate ordination documents or the framed university degree proudly displayed. But are there living letters? The confirmation of one's ministry lies in the effects of that ministry in human lives."*

## 2. The Redeemed Christian Is Changed at the Heart Level (v. 3)

> *"...written not with ink but by the Spirit of the living God, not on tablets of stone but on tablets of flesh, that is, of the heart."* (2 Corinthians 3:3)

The hearts of the Corinthian Christians were changed by the Holy Spirit as a result of Paul's preaching of the gospel. When we put together Paul's imagery here — heart of stone becoming heart of flesh, the Spirit writing on the heart — we see that these are allusions to the core promise of the New Covenant. Paul mentions the New Covenant explicitly in verse 6, but its essence is right here:

> *"Behold, the days are coming, says the Lord, when I will make a new covenant with the house of Israel and with the house of Judah... I will put My law in their minds, and write it on their hearts; and I will be their God, and they shall be My people."* (Jeremiah 31:31–33)

And Ezekiel, about a hundred years later, describing the same covenant:

> *"I will give you a new heart and put a new spirit within you; I will take the heart of stone out of your flesh and give you a heart of flesh. I will put My Spirit within you and cause you to walk in My statutes."* (Ezekiel 36:26–27)

The result of the New Covenant is a changed heart that rejoices to do the will of God. What happened to the Corinthians happens to us as the Spirit changes our hearts in response to the proclamation of the gospel of Jesus Christ.

The proclamation of the gospel is like a man standing in front of a valley of dry bones. That is Ezekiel 37 — right after the New Covenant passage. God says: Ezekiel, preach my word to these bones. And Ezekiel preaches, and an army that cannot be numbered is raised up for the Lord. Not because they understood the sermon — because the Spirit of God worked through the word proclaimed. That is what the Lord has done for us.

## 3. The Redeemed Christian's Confidence Is in Christ Alone (vv. 4–5)

> *"And we have such trust through Christ toward God. Not that we are sufficient of ourselves to think of anything as being from ourselves, but our sufficiency is from God."* (2 Corinthians 3:4–5)

Paul's confidence doesn't come from anything in himself. His confidence that the Corinthians are Christ's credentials is not based on his skills, eloquence, or rhetorical ability. Praise God that your spiritual destiny does not depend on my abilities.

> *"And I, brethren, when I came to you, did not come with excellence of speech or of wisdom declaring to you the testimony of God. For I determined not to know anything among you except Jesus Christ and Him crucified. I was with you in weakness, in fear, and in much trembling, and my speech and my preaching were not with persuasive words of human wisdom, but in demonstration of the Spirit and of power, that your faith should not be in the wisdom of men but in the power of God."* (1 Corinthians 2:1–5)

We tend to use the word "sufficient" to mean "the bare minimum required." But that is not how the Bible uses the word. In Scripture, sufficiency means *all that is needed* — not the minimum, but the whole. Christ is sufficient. He is not the bare minimum — He is all that is needed.

If a pastor thinks that people coming to Christ or growing in Christ depends on the quality of his words and the skill with which he says them, the only reasonable response would be despair — because the burden is too great. But our confidence is not in ourselves; our confidence is in Christ and Him crucified. None of us should insert ourselves into the center of the proclamation of the gospel. Christ is sufficient.

Paul demonstrates this in Philippians 1, where he hears that some people are preaching the gospel accurately with the intent of getting him in trouble, and he says: *"What then? Only that in every way, whether in pretense or in truth, Christ is preached; and in this I rejoice, yes, and will rejoice."* He doesn't care about his own reputation as long as the true gospel is proclaimed — because the Spirit changes people through that.

Who or what is your confidence today? What are you clinging to — yourself, or Jesus Christ? Paul says in Philippians 3:

> *"But what things were gain to me, these I have counted loss for Christ. Yet indeed I also count all things loss for the excellence of the knowledge of Christ Jesus my Lord... that I may gain Christ and be found in Him, not having my own righteousness, which is from the law, but that which is through faith in Christ, the righteousness which is from God by faith."* (Philippians 3:7–9)

## 4. The Redeemed Christian Has Life by the Spirit (v. 6)

> *"...who also made us sufficient as ministers of the new covenant, not of the letter but of the Spirit; for the letter kills, but the Spirit gives life."* (2 Corinthians 3:6)

Note: in verse 6, the word "letter" is not the word for an epistle — it is the word for a single letter of the alphabet. Paul is using it to refer to the Mosaic Covenant, the law, as opposed to the Spirit who empowers obedience to that law.

The letter kills when used improperly — as a set of rules to obey in order to establish one's own righteousness before God, apart from the empowering work of the Spirit. Have you ever tried to be so good that God would love you solely because of what you do? When you do that, you realize very quickly that you fall far short of the glory of God. That is what death is. The law kills any sense of self-sufficiency and pride, any idea that we can better ourselves by our own efforts.

> *"Therefore by the deeds of the law no flesh will be justified in His sight, for by the law is the knowledge of sin."* (Romans 3:20)

But Paul is not saying the law is bad. The letter kills only when used *improperly* — as if obedience to rules could establish righteousness before God. The Spirit works with the law. Without the law, there is no gospel. When the law kills us — when any sense of pride is gone, when we have nothing to bring — then we are ready. Then Christ makes us alive.

Friends, if any of you think that you are good enough for God — you need to die to yourself. There is no hope while you stand in that position. Only when the law has killed you, only when any sense of pride is gone, are you ready to be made alive by the Lord Jesus Christ.

*"It is the Spirit who gives life; the flesh profits nothing."* (John 6:63)

## Conclusion: The True Badge of Authenticity

In the end, Paul reminds the Corinthians that the true badge of authenticity in the Christian life is not framing papers and titles or human credentials — it is the transformed life. The redeemed Christian is a living letter from Christ, written not with ink but with the Spirit, not on stone but on a fleshly heart — a person who has been given a new heart that responds to the Lord and His gospel.

And that means our sufficiency is in Christ and nothing else. Who is your sufficiency? Who are you looking to for your hope — yourself, or Jesus Christ? Because again, these are the only two choices we have in life. Our confidence is in Christ and in Him crucified, risen from the dead, reigning at the right hand of the Father — whom we will see as He is, for we shall be like Him.
